text: The BGP Finite State Machine (FSM) defines the lifecycle of a BGP session. The key states are: Idle — BGP is not attempting to connect, either because it is waiting for a retry timer or because the session was administratively shut down. Connect — BGP is waiting for the TCP connection to complete. Active — BGP is actively trying to connect by initiating TCP connections. OpenSent — TCP connection established, BGP Open message sent, waiting for peer Open. OpenConfirm — Open messages exchanged, waiting for Keepalive. Established — BGP session is fully operational. When troubleshooting, Idle with tcp_connect_failed means the TCP connection never succeeded — check underlay. Active means TCP is being attempted but not succeeding — check reachability and port 179. OpenSent/OpenConfirm stuck means BGP parameter mismatch — check AS numbers, hold timers, capabilities.

BGP route reflectors (RR) are used in iBGP networks to avoid the full mesh requirement. A route reflector reflects routes received from iBGP clients to other clients and non-clients. In a spine-leaf fabric, spine switches typically act as route reflectors for leaf iBGP clients. When a route reflector fails, all clients lose their iBGP-learned routes simultaneously. This appears as multiple session failures with a common cause — the RR peer IP. The correct diagnosis approach for correlated RR failures: (1) identify the common peer IP across all failing sessions, (2) verify reachability to that IP, (3) check the RR BGP process health, (4) allow automatic reconvergence — do not reset individual client sessions. Route reflector redundancy is achieved by configuring two RRs per cluster with the same cluster-id.

BGP prefixes go through a lifecycle: origination (network statement or redistribution), advertisement (UPDATE message to peers), installation (in peer RIB after import policy), and withdrawal (WITHDRAW message when the prefix is removed). When a peer stops advertising a prefix, the receiving router sends a route withdrawal to its peers. Common causes of unexpected withdrawal: the originating router lost the route from its RIB (interface down, redistributed protocol removed the route), an export policy filtered the prefix, or the BGP session went down. When troubleshooting prefix loss: first check if the prefix exists on the originating device, then check if it is being advertised, then check for policy filtering, then check session state. Withdrawn prefixes appear in BGP history as route_removed events.
